title: The Little Prince
author: Antoine de Saint-Exupéry (retold)
description: A philosophical tale about a young prince who travels from planet to planet, learning about life, love, and human nature.
descriptionZh: 小王子的星际旅行，一个关于生命、爱与人性的哲理故事。改编简化版。

chapters.title: The Drawing
paragraphs: Once when I was six years old, I saw a beautiful picture in a book about the jungle. It was a picture of a boa constrictor swallowing a wild animal. The book said: "Boa constrictors swallow their prey whole, without chewing it. After that they are not able to move, and they sleep for six months."
I thought about this for a long time. Then, with a coloured pencil, I made my first drawing. It was a picture of a boa constrictor that had swallowed an elephant. It looked like a hat.
I showed my masterpiece to the grown-ups. I asked them if my drawing frightened them. They answered: "Why should anyone be frightened by a hat?"

My drawing was not a picture of a hat. It was a picture of a boa constrictor digesting an elephant. So I made another drawing. This time I drew the inside, so the grown-ups could see clearly. They always need to have things explained.
The grown-ups told me to stop drawing boa constrictors and focus on geography, history, arithmetic, and grammar instead. That is why, at the age of six, I gave up a wonderful career as a painter.

I had been discouraged by the failure of my Drawing Number One and my Drawing Number Two. Grown-ups never understand anything by themselves, and it is tiresome for children to be always explaining things to them.

chapters.title: The Pilot and the Little Prince
paragraphs: So I lived my life alone, without anyone that I could really talk to. I became a pilot and flew aeroplanes. I flew all over the world. And geography was indeed very useful to me.
I have lived a great deal among grown-ups. I have seen them close up. That has not much improved my opinion of them.

Whenever I met one of them who seemed clear-sighted, I would show him my Drawing Number One. I wanted to find out if he truly understood things. But the answer was always: "That is a hat." Then I would never talk to that person about boa constrictors, or jungles, or stars. I would talk about bridge, golf, politics, and neckties. And the grown-up would be very pleased to have met such a sensible man.

I lived this way, alone, until six years ago when I had an accident in the Sahara Desert. Something was broken in my engine. I had neither a mechanic nor passengers with me, so I set out to try to do the repair work alone.
It was a matter of life or death for me. I had hardly enough drinking water to last a week.
The first night I went to sleep on the sand, a thousand miles from any human habitation. I was more isolated than a sailor on a raft in the middle of the ocean.
So you can imagine my amazement, at sunrise, when I was woken by a strange little voice. It said: "If you please, draw me a sheep!"

chapters.title: Draw Me a Sheep
paragraphs: I jumped to my feet as if I had been struck by lightning. I rubbed my eyes. I looked carefully around me. And I saw an extraordinary small person standing there, looking at me seriously.
He did not look at all like a child lost in the middle of the desert, a thousand miles from any inhabited place. When at last I managed to speak, I said to him: "But... what are you doing here?"
He repeated, very slowly, as if it were a matter of great importance: "If you please, draw me a sheep."
I did not understand. I told him I did not know how to draw sheep. "That doesn't matter," he said. "Draw me a sheep."

So I drew one of the only two pictures I knew how to make — the boa constrictor from the outside. I was astonished to hear the little fellow say: "No, no! I do not want an elephant inside a boa constrictor. A boa constrictor is very dangerous, and an elephant is very bulky. Where I live, everything is very small. What I need is a sheep. Draw me a sheep."

He knew what my drawing was! So I drew a sheep. He looked at it carefully. "No, this one is already very sickly. Make me another." I drew another. My friend smiled gently. "This is not a sheep. This is a ram. It has horns."
So I drew yet another sheep. But it was rejected too, like the others. "This one is too old."
By this time my patience was exhausted, because I was in a hurry to start work on repairing my engine. So I tossed off a quick drawing. "This is the box," I said. "The sheep you asked for is inside."

To my surprise, the face of my young judge lit up. "That is exactly the way I wanted it! Do you think this sheep will need a great deal of grass?" And so it was that I made the acquaintance of the little prince.

chapters.title: The Little Prince's Planet
paragraphs: It took me a long time to learn where he came from. The little prince asked me many questions, but he never seemed to hear mine. It was from words dropped by chance that, little by little, everything was revealed to me.
The first time he saw my aeroplane, he asked: "What is that thing?" I told him it was not a thing. It was an aeroplane. It flies. I was proud to tell him that I could fly.
"What! You fell from the sky?" "Yes," I said modestly. "Oh! That is funny!" And the little prince broke into a lovely peal of laughter.

I soon learned that he came from a very small planet, hardly any bigger than a house. This did not really surprise me much. I knew very well that besides the great planets like Earth, Jupiter, Mars, and Venus, there are also hundreds of others, some of which are so tiny that one has a hard time seeing them through a telescope.
When an astronomer discovers one of these tiny planets, he gives it a number instead of a name. He calls it, for example, "Asteroid 325."
I have reason to believe that the planet the little prince came from was Asteroid B-612. This asteroid had only been seen once through a telescope, in 1909, by a Turkish astronomer.

I tell you all these details about Asteroid B-612 because grown-ups love numbers. When you tell them about a new friend, they never ask you the important questions. They never say: "What does his voice sound like? What games does he love best?" Instead they ask: "How old is he? How much does he weigh? How much money does his father make?" Only from these numbers do they think they have learned anything about him.

chapters.title: The Baobabs
paragraphs: Each day I learned something new about the little prince's planet, about his departure from it, and about his journey. It all came very slowly, as it happened to come from his thoughts.
It was in this way that I heard, on the third day, about the terrible danger of the baobabs.
"Is it true that sheep eat little bushes?" the little prince asked me one morning.
"Yes, that is true."

"Ah! Then I am glad!" For the little prince explained to me that on his planet there were terrible seeds — the seeds of the baobab. The soil of the planet was full of them. A baobab is a very large tree, and if you are too late in pulling it up, it can never be got rid of. It spreads over the entire planet and its roots can split it apart.

"It is a question of discipline," the little prince said to me later. "When you have finished your morning washing, you must tend your planet carefully. You must pull up the baobabs regularly, as soon as you can tell them apart from the rose bushes. They look very much alike when they are very young. It is very tedious work, but very easy."
He said to me seriously: "Children, watch out for the baobabs!" He drew a very beautiful picture of baobabs to make the point clear.
"Why is this so important?" I asked. "Because," he replied, "if you let bad habits grow too large, they become impossible to remove. It is the same with baobabs."

chapters.title: The Sunset
paragraphs: On the fourth day, the little prince said to me: "I am very fond of sunsets. Come, let us go look at a sunset now."
"But we must wait," I said. "Wait? For what?" "For the sunset. We must wait until it is time."
At first the little prince seemed very surprised, and then he laughed at himself. "I always think I am at home!" he said.

And indeed. When it is noon in the United States, the sun is setting over France. If you could fly to France in one minute, you could watch the sunset. Unfortunately, France is too far away. But on the little prince's tiny planet, he only had to move his chair a few steps. He could watch the sunset whenever he wanted.
"One day," the little prince told me, "I saw the sunset forty-four times!" And a little later he added: "You know, when one is very sad, one loves sunsets."
"Were you so sad, then?" I asked. "On the day of the forty-four sunsets?"
But the little prince did not answer.

chapters.title: The Flower
paragraphs: On the fifth day, again thanks to the sheep, the secret of the little prince's life was revealed to me. He asked me suddenly, without warning: "A sheep — if it eats little bushes, does it eat flowers too?"
"A sheep eats anything it finds." "Even flowers that have thorns?" "Yes, even flowers that have thorns."
"Then what use are the thorns?"

I did not know. At that moment I was very busy trying to unscrew a bolt that had got stuck in my engine. I was worried because my breakdown was beginning to appear very serious. My drinking water was running out.

"What use are the thorns?" The little prince never let go of a question once he had asked it. I was irritated by the stuck bolt and answered without thinking: "Thorns are of no use at all. Flowers have thorns just out of meanness!"

There was a moment of silence. Then the little prince said, in a voice full of anger: "I don't believe you! Flowers are weak creatures. They have thorns to protect themselves. They think their thorns make them terrible. And you think thorns are of no use!"
I said nothing. I was looking at the bolt. But the little prince's words disturbed my thoughts.

"If someone loves a flower, of which just one single blossom grows in all the millions and millions of stars, it is enough to make him happy just to look at the stars. He can say to himself: 'My flower is somewhere out there.' But if the sheep eats the flower, then for him it is as if all the stars suddenly went dark. And you think that is not important?"

He could not say anything more. He suddenly broke into sobs. Night had fallen. I had let my tools drop from my hands. What did my hammer, my bolt, or thirst matter to me? On a star, on a planet — my planet, the Earth — there was a little prince to be comforted. I took him in my arms and rocked him.

chapters.title: The Rose
paragraphs: I soon learned more about the little prince's flower. On his planet, there had always been very simple flowers — with a single ring of petals — that took up no room and bothered nobody. They appeared one morning in the grass and by evening they had faded away.

But one day, from a seed blown from nobody knew where, a new sprout appeared. The little prince watched over this sprout very carefully. It might have been a new kind of baobab. But the bush soon stopped growing and began to make a flower.

The little prince watched the huge bud getting ready to bloom. He could see that a great miracle was being prepared. But the flower took her time. She chose her colours with care. She adjusted her petals one by one. She did not wish to come out looking crumpled like a poppy.

Then one morning, at the hour of sunrise, she suddenly showed herself. After all that careful preparation, she yawned and said: "Ah! I am scarcely awake. I beg that you will excuse me. My petals are still all in disorder."
She was very beautiful. "How lovely you are!" said the little prince.
"Am I not?" the flower responded sweetly. "And I was born at the same moment as the sun." She was not very modest. But she was so beautiful!
"I think it is time for breakfast," she soon added. "If you would be so kind as to think of my needs..." And the little prince went to find a watering can of fresh water to give her.

So she had begun to torment him with her vanity. She was a little dramatic. One day she told the little prince about her four thorns: "Let the tigers come with their claws!" But there were no tigers on his planet.

The little prince loved his flower very much, but he was too young to understand her. He took her words too seriously and became very unhappy. "I ought not to have listened to her," he told me one day. "One must never listen to flowers. One must look at them and breathe in their fragrance. My flower filled my planet with her sweet smell, but I did not know how to enjoy it."

"I should have judged her by her actions, not by her words," he continued. "She cast her fragrance and her light over me. I ought never to have run away from her. I should have guessed at the tenderness behind her poor tricks. Flowers are so contradictory! But I was too young to know how to love her."

chapters.title: The Departure
paragraphs: I believe the little prince used a flock of wild birds to make his escape from his planet. On the morning of his departure, he put his planet in perfect order. He carefully pulled up the last little baobab sprouts.

He believed he would never come back. But on this last morning, all these familiar tasks seemed very precious to him. And when he watered the flower for the last time and prepared to cover her with a glass globe, he felt like crying.
"Goodbye," he said to the flower.
But she did not answer. "Goodbye," he said again.
The flower coughed. But it was not because she had a cold. "I have been silly," she said to him at last. "I am sorry. Try to be happy."
He was surprised by the absence of any complaints. He stood there, quite bewildered, the glass globe held in mid-air. He did not understand this quiet gentleness.

"Of course I love you," the flower said to him. "It was my fault that you never knew it. But that is not important now. You have been just as foolish as I. Try to be happy. Put that glass globe down. I don't want it anymore."
"But the wind..." "My cold is not that bad. The cool night air will do me good. I am a flower."

"But the animals..." "I must put up with two or three caterpillars if I wish to become friends with the butterflies. I am told they are very beautiful. Besides, who will come to visit me? You will be far away. As for the large animals, I am not afraid of them. I have my thorns."
And she showed her four thorns. Then she added: "Don't linger like this. It is annoying. You have decided to go. Now go!"
She did not want him to see her crying. She was such a proud flower.

chapters.title: The King
paragraphs: The little prince found himself in the neighbourhood of Asteroids 325, 326, 327, 328, 329, and 330. He began visiting them, one by one, in order to learn and to find something to do.
The first planet was inhabited by a king. He was dressed in royal purple and sat upon a very simple yet majestic throne.
"Ah! Here is a subject!" cried the king when he saw the little prince coming. The little prince wondered how the king could recognize him, since he had never seen him before.
He did not know that, to kings, the whole world is very simple. Everyone is a subject.
"Come closer so that I may see you better," said the king, who was very proud to be a king for somebody at last.
The little prince looked around for somewhere to sit, but the entire planet was taken up by the king's magnificent robe. So he remained standing, and since he was tired, he yawned.
"It is contrary to etiquette to yawn in the presence of a king," said the monarch. "I forbid you to yawn."
"I can't help it," replied the little prince, embarrassed. "I have come on a long journey, and I have had no sleep."
"Ah, then," the king said. "I order you to yawn. I have not seen anyone yawn for years. Yawns are a curiosity to me. Come now! Yawn again! It is an order!"

The little prince blushed. He could not yawn to order. "Well, then," said the king. "I order you sometimes to yawn and sometimes to..." He sputtered a little and seemed upset. For the king insisted that his authority be respected. He would not tolerate disobedience. He was an absolute monarch.

But he was also a very good man, and he made all his orders reasonable. "If I ordered a general to fly from one flower to another like a butterfly," he would say, "and if the general did not carry out my order, it would not be the general's fault. It would be mine."
"May I sit down?" asked the little prince timidly.
"I order you to sit down," the king hastily replied, and pulled a fold of his great robe closer to him.

The little prince sat and thought. This planet was very small. Over what could this king really rule? "Sire," he said, "I beg you to pardon me for asking a question." "I order you to ask me a question," the king hurried to say.
"Sire, over what do you rule?" "Over everything," said the king with great simplicity.
The little prince looked around. The planet was very small indeed. "And the stars obey you?" "Certainly," said the king. "They obey instantly. I do not permit disobedience."
"I should like to see a sunset," said the little prince. "Do me a favour. Order the sun to set."

"If I ordered a general to fly like a butterfly, it would be the general's fault if he did not obey. I only give reasonable orders. You shall have your sunset. I shall demand it. But I shall wait for the right conditions, because that is the art of ruling."
"When will that be?" asked the little prince. "That will be about... about... that will be this evening, about twenty minutes to eight. And you will see how well I am obeyed."

The little prince yawned. He was feeling sorry that he could not have his sunset at the moment he wanted it. And besides, he was already becoming a little bored. "I have nothing more to do here," he said to the king. "I am going to leave."
"Do not leave!" said the king, who was very proud of having a subject. "Do not leave. I will make you a Minister!"
"A Minister of what?" "Of... of Justice!" "But there is nobody here to judge!"
"I appoint you my Ambassador!" the king called out after him as the little prince departed. "Grown-ups are very strange," the little prince said to himself as he continued on his journey.
